category_title: Quiet, independent students with LD who struggle with idea generation and persistence in writing
minimal_context: Tyrone is sitting at his desk during independent writing time in a noisy classroom, tasked with analyzing a prompt and writing a first draft.
dialogue:
TUTOR: We have thirty minutes for independent writing now. Please analyze the prompt from the mini-lesson and start writing your first draft.

STUDENT: (Sits quietly at his desk looking at the blank paper without writing anything.)

TUTOR: How is your writing going?

STUDENT: Great.

TUTOR: Alright, keep working on getting your ideas down.

STUDENT: (Continues to sit quietly without being able to start writing. He looks around the noisy classroom, puts his pencil down, and gives up on generating ideas.)

options.A: Remain available without forcing interaction, first simply sitting near the student, then speaking quietly and gently touching his shoulder.
options.B: Bring digital storytelling into the classroom, allowing the student to draw pictures and add his own voice.
options.C: Provide continuous access to competing sensory items (e.g., a textured ball) during writing time to reduce the occurrence of giving up.
options.D: Establish a norm where disagreement is acceptable and provide a platform for the evidence-based exchange of viewpoints.
